subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Science: How a Generator Makes Electricity
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Science strand: a generator produces electricity by using motion, such as spinning magnets near coils of wire, to convert mechanical energy into electrical energy.
subjects.resourceLabel: TVO Learn: Grade 4 Science and Technology

quiz.q: What does a generator produce?
Does a generator use motion, such as spinning magnets, to produce electricity?
options: No, generators never use any motion

quiz.q: What type of energy does a generator convert into electrical energy?
Why might a generator be useful during a power outage?
options: Generators can only be used when regular power is already working
Generators are never useful during a power outage
This concept has no connection to electricity
It can produce electricity when the regular power supply is unavailable

quiz.q: Why is understanding how a generator works helpful for understanding renewable energy, like wind power?
options: Many renewable energy sources, like wind turbines, also use motion to generate electricity
Wind power never actually produces any electricity
This concept has no relevance to science
Generators have no connection to renewable energy

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Social Studies: Global Trade Organizations and Agreements
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Social Studies strand: countries often join trade organizations or sign agreements to set shared rules for buying and selling goods internationally, helping trade run smoothly.

quiz.q: What do countries often join to set shared rules for international trade?
options: A concept unrelated to global trade

quiz.q: Do trade agreements help set rules for buying and selling goods between countries?
options: Trade agreements only apply within one single country
No, trade agreements have no connection to buying and selling goods

quiz.q: Can trade organizations help trade run more smoothly between countries?
options: Trade organizations have no connection to trade at all
A concept unrelated to international relations
No, trade organizations always make trade more difficult

quiz.q: Why might countries want shared rules for international trade instead of each having completely different rules?
options: Shared rules can make trading goods more predictable and fair for everyone involved
This concept has no connection to global trade
Countries never actually trade goods with each other
Shared trade rules never actually help anyone

quiz.q: Why might joining a trade organization benefit a country’s economy?
options: This concept has no relevance to social studies
Trade organizations only exist to limit a country’s economy
Joining a trade organization never benefits a country’s economy
It can open up new markets and opportunities to sell and buy goods

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Review: Vocabulary, Grammar, and Reading Skills (Days 81-89)
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Language strand review: students revisit portmanteau words, active and passive voice, symbolism, speech writing, oxymorons, correlative conjunctions, author credibility, etymology, and persuasive letters.

quiz.q: A portmanteau word blends the sounds and meanings of how many words?
In the active voice, does the subject perform the action or receive it?
What do we call it when an author uses an object to represent a deeper idea?
What do we call a phrase that combines two contradictory words, like jumbo shrimp?
options: A concept unrelated to figurative language

quiz.q: What do we call the study of where words come from?

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Review: Decimals, Fractions, and Data (Days 81-89)
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Math strand review: students revisit multiplying and dividing decimals, classifying quadrilaterals, stem-and-leaf plots, adding mixed numbers, converting mass, probability as a fraction, algebraic expressions, and simple interest.

quiz.q: What is 0.4 times 3?
What is 6.4 divided by 2?
How many equal sides does a rhombus have?
What is 2 and 1 fourth plus 1 and 2 fourths?
What is the probability, as a fraction, of flipping heads on a coin?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Review: Ecosystems, Earth and Space, and Physical Science (Days 81-89)
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Science strand review: students revisit food webs, comets and asteroids, weather fronts, air pollution, desert adaptations, chemical reactions, eclipses, invasive species, and generators.
subjects.resourceLabel: TVO Learn: Grade 4 Science and Technology

quiz.q: Does a food web show a single path of energy, or many connected food chains?
What is a comet mostly made of?
What do we call the place where two different air masses meet?
What happens during a solar eclipse?
options: Nothing happens in the sky at all
Earth passes between the Sun and Moon
The Moon passes between the Sun and Earth

quiz.q: What does a generator produce?

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Review: Ancient Societies, Government, and Global Connections (Days 81-89)
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Social Studies strand review: students revisit the Aztec civilization, museums, Canadian peacekeeping, comparing government systems, the United Nations, immigration policy, land acknowledgements, currency history, and global trade.

quiz.q: What was the name of the Aztec capital city?
options: A concept unrelated to the Aztecs

quiz.q: What do we call soldiers sent to help maintain peace in a conflict region?
options: A concept unrelated to international relations

quiz.q: In a democracy, how are leaders typically chosen?
options: A single king or queen decides

quiz.q: What do we call a statement recognizing the Indigenous Peoples connected to a piece of land?
options: A concept unrelated to Indigenous history

quiz.q: What do countries often join to set shared rules for international trade?
options: A concept unrelated to global trade

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Grammar: Verb Tenses — Past, Present, and Future
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Language strand: verb tense tells when an action happens — past tense describes something that already happened, present tense describes something happening now, and future tense describes something that will happen.

quiz.q: Which verb tense describes an action that already happened?
Which sentence uses the future tense?
options: She will walk to school tomorrow.
She walks to school every day.

quiz.q: Which sentence uses the present tense?
options: He will play soccer next Saturday.

quiz.q: Why is it important to use consistent verb tense throughout a piece of writing?
options: Mixing tenses always makes writing easier to understand
This concept has no connection to writing
It helps the reader clearly understand when events are happening
Verb tense never affects how clear a sentence is

quiz.q: Which sentence correctly uses the past tense of the verb jump?
options: Yesterday, the frog jumped into the pond.
Yesterday, the frog jumps into the pond.
Yesterday, the frog will jump into the pond.

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Fractions: Subtracting Mixed Numbers
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Math strand: students learn to subtract one mixed number from another, such as finding 3 and 3 fourths minus 1 and 1 fourth, by subtracting the whole numbers and the fractions separately.

quiz.q: What is 3 and 3 fourths minus 1 and 1 fourth?
What is 5 and 2 fifths minus 2 and 1 fifth?
What is 4 and 5 sixths minus 2 and 2 sixths?
When subtracting mixed numbers, what should you subtract first?
options: Only the fractions, ignoring the whole numbers completely
Neither part should ever be subtracted
The whole numbers and the fractions can each be subtracted separately
Only the whole numbers, ignoring the fractions completely

quiz.q: What is 6 and 3 fourths minus 4 and 1 fourth?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Science: Structures: Shell, Frame, and Solid Structures
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Science strand: structures can be built as shell structures with a strong outer covering and hollow inside, frame structures made of connected beams or rods, or solid structures that are filled all the way through.
subjects.resourceLabel: TVO Learn: Grade 4 Science and Technology

quiz.q: What do we call a structure with a strong outer covering and a hollow inside?
What do we call a structure made of connected beams or rods?
Is a solid structure, such as a dam, filled all the way through?
options: No, solid structures are always hollow
Solid structures have no material inside them at all

quiz.q: Why might an egg be considered an example of a shell structure?
options: This concept has no relevance to science
Its strong outer covering protects the hollow space inside
An egg has no connection to structures
An egg is completely solid all the way through

quiz.q: Why might engineers choose a frame structure, like a bicycle frame, instead of a solid structure?
options: Frame structures are always heavier than solid structures
This concept has no connection to structures
Frame structures are never used for anything real
A frame can be strong while using less material and being lighter

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Social Studies: Ancient Inca Civilization
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Social Studies strand: the Inca civilization built a large empire along the Andes Mountains in South America, known for its capital city Cusco, extensive road system, and the mountaintop city of Machu Picchu.

quiz.q: Where did the Inca civilization build its empire?
options: Along the Andes Mountains in South America
A concept unrelated to ancient civilizations

quiz.q: What was the name of the Inca capital city?
options: A concept unrelated to the Inca

quiz.q: What famous mountaintop city did the Inca build?
options: A concept unrelated to the Inca

quiz.q: Why might the Inca’s extensive road system have been important to their empire?
options: Roads have no connection to ancient empires
This concept has no relevance to social studies
It likely helped connect distant regions for trade, travel, and communication
The Inca never built any roads at all

quiz.q: Why do historians consider the Inca’s ability to build in the mountains impressive?
options: This concept has no relevance to social studies
Building in mountains has no connection to history
The Inca never built anything in the mountains
They constructed lasting stone structures on difficult, high-altitude terrain

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Reading: Understanding Foreshadowing
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Language strand: foreshadowing is when an author gives a hint or clue early in a story about something that will happen later, building suspense for the reader.

quiz.q: What do we call a hint an author gives early in a story about something that will happen later?
Does foreshadowing usually appear near the beginning or end of a story?
options: Foreshadowing never appears anywhere in a story

quiz.q: What feeling can foreshadowing help build in a reader?
Why might an author use foreshadowing instead of simply stating what will happen?
options: Authors should never give any hints about future events
It keeps readers curious and engaged as they wait to see what happens
This concept has no connection to storytelling
Foreshadowing never affects how a reader feels

quiz.q: If a story shows dark storm clouds gathering just before a character sets sail, what might this foreshadow?
options: Trouble or danger ahead on the journey
Nothing at all connected to the plot
A concept unrelated to the story

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Number Sense: Representing Numbers in Expanded Form
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Math strand: students learn to write a number in expanded form by showing the value of each digit, such as writing 3,482 as 3,000 plus 400 plus 80 plus 2.

quiz.q: What is 3,482 written in expanded form?
What is 5,791 written in expanded form?
What number is represented by 2,000 + 300 + 40 + 5?
Why is expanded form useful for understanding a number like 4,672?
options: It shows the value that each digit represents based on its place
Expanded form always hides the value of a number
Expanded form never shows any useful information
This concept has no connection to place value

quiz.q: What number is represented by 6,000 + 200 + 10 + 9?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Science: Matter: Methods for Separating Mixtures
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Science strand: mixtures can be separated using methods such as filtering, which uses a strainer to catch solid pieces, or evaporation, which lets a liquid dry up and leaves behind the solid that was dissolved in it.
subjects.resourceLabel: TVO Learn: Grade 4 Science and Technology

quiz.q: What method uses a strainer to catch solid pieces out of a mixture?
What method lets a liquid dry up to leave behind a dissolved solid?
Could you use filtering to separate sand from water?
options: No, filtering never separates sand from water

quiz.q: Why might evaporation be a good method for separating salt from salt water?
options: This concept has no relevance to science
Evaporation always removes the salt instead of the water
The water dries up and leaves the solid salt behind
Evaporation has no connection to separating mixtures

quiz.q: Why is it useful to know different ways to separate mixtures?
options: It helps us recover or clean useful materials from a combined mixture
Mixtures can never actually be separated
Separating mixtures never has any real use
This concept has no connection to matter

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Social Studies: Canada’s Constitution and the Charter of Rights and Freedoms
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Social Studies strand: Canada’s Constitution is the country’s highest set of laws, and it includes the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, which protects basic rights such as freedom of speech and equality for all Canadians.

quiz.q: What do we call the country’s highest set of laws?
What part of the Constitution protects basic rights like freedom of speech?
options: A concept unrelated to Canadian law
The Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms

quiz.q: Does the Charter protect equality for all Canadians?
options: Only some Canadians have any rights at all
No, the Charter protects no one

quiz.q: Why might it be important for a country to have a constitution?
options: This concept has no connection to government
A constitution never affects how a country is governed
It sets out the basic rules and rights that guide how the country is governed
Countries never need any basic laws at all

quiz.q: Why might freedom of speech, protected by the Charter, be important in a democracy?
options: Freedom of speech has no connection to democracy
Citizens are never allowed to share opinions in a democracy
It allows citizens to share their opinions and ideas openly
This concept has no relevance to social studies

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Vocabulary: Shades of Meaning
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Language strand: many words have similar meanings but different shades of meaning, such as happy, glad, and thrilled, which all describe positive feelings but with different levels of intensity.

quiz.q: What do we call words with similar meanings but different levels of intensity, like happy, glad, and thrilled?
Which word suggests the strongest level of happiness?
options: A concept unrelated to shades of meaning

quiz.q: Which word suggests a milder feeling of being upset than furious?
options: A concept unrelated to shades of meaning

quiz.q: Why might a writer choose the word enormous instead of big?
options: Word choice never changes how a sentence feels
Enormous conveys a stronger, more precise shade of meaning
Enormous and big always mean exactly opposite things
This concept has no connection to vocabulary

quiz.q: Which word shows the mildest shade of meaning for cold?
options: A concept unrelated to shades of meaning

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Financial Literacy: Adding and Subtracting Money Amounts
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Math strand: students learn to add and subtract amounts of money with dollars and cents, such as finding the total cost of two items or the change owed after a purchase.

quiz.q: What is 4 dollars 25 cents plus 2 dollars 50 cents?
What is 10 dollars minus 3 dollars 40 cents?
If a book costs 8 dollars 15 cents and a pen costs 1 dollar 20 cents, what is the total cost?
Why is it important to line up dollars and cents correctly when adding money amounts?
options: Dollars and cents can be mixed up with no effect on the answer
This concept has no connection to adding money
Lining up money amounts never matters
It keeps the place value correct so the total is accurate

quiz.q: If you pay 20 dollars for an item that costs 14 dollars 75 cents, how much change should you receive?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Science: Energy: Potential and Kinetic Energy
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Science strand: potential energy is stored energy an object has because of its position, such as a ball held up high, while kinetic energy is the energy of motion, such as that same ball falling or rolling.
subjects.resourceLabel: TVO Learn: Grade 4 Science and Technology

quiz.q: What do we call stored energy an object has because of its position?
What do we call the energy of motion?
Does a ball held up high have potential energy?
options: No, a held ball never has any energy
Only moving objects can have any energy at all

quiz.q: Why does a ball rolling down a hill have kinetic energy?
options: A rolling ball never actually has any energy
This concept has no relevance to science
Kinetic energy has no connection to motion
It is in motion, and motion is what kinetic energy describes

quiz.q: Why might a roller coaster at the top of a hill have a lot of potential energy?
options: This concept has no relevance to science
Its high position stores energy that can convert to motion as it descends
Height has no connection to potential energy
A roller coaster never has any stored energy

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Social Studies: How Canadians Vote: Federal Elections
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Social Studies strand: in a federal election, Canadian citizens who are eighteen or older can vote for a candidate to represent their riding in the House of Commons, helping decide who leads the country.

quiz.q: How old must a Canadian citizen typically be to vote in a federal election?
What do voters choose in a federal election?
options: A candidate to represent their riding

quiz.q: Where do elected federal representatives sit to make decisions for the country?
Why is voting an important responsibility for eligible citizens in a democracy?
options: This concept has no connection to democracy
Voting never actually affects who leads a country
Citizens have no role in choosing their government
It gives citizens a voice in choosing who leads and represents them

quiz.q: Why might elections be held regularly instead of only once?
options: Elections never need to happen more than once
This concept has no relevance to social studies
Regular elections let citizens choose new leaders and hold them accountable
Leaders are never accountable to citizens in a democracy

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Grammar: Sentence Fragments and Run-on Sentences
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Language strand: a sentence fragment is an incomplete sentence missing a subject or verb, while a run-on sentence incorrectly joins two or more complete sentences together without proper punctuation.

quiz.q: What do we call an incomplete sentence that is missing a subject or verb?
What do we call two or more complete sentences incorrectly joined without proper punctuation?
Which of these is a sentence fragment?
options: She ran to the store, and then she came home.

quiz.q: Why is it important to fix run-on sentences in your writing?
options: Readers always understand run-on sentences perfectly
This concept has no connection to writing clearly
It helps readers understand where one idea ends and another begins
Run-on sentences never cause any confusion for readers

quiz.q: Which sentence correctly fixes this run-on: I like pizza I like pasta too?
options: I like pizza, and I like pasta too.
I like, pizza I like pasta too.
I like pizza I like pasta too

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Data Management: Choosing an Appropriate Graph
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Math strand: students learn that different graphs suit different data, such as bar graphs for comparing categories, line graphs for showing change over time, and circle graphs for showing parts of a whole.

quiz.q: Which type of graph is best for comparing amounts in different categories?
Which type of graph is best for showing how something changes over time?
Which type of graph is best for showing parts of a whole, like percentages of a budget?
Why might a scientist use a line graph to track a plant’s height over several weeks?
options: A line graph clearly shows how a value changes over time
This concept has no connection to data management
Line graphs never show any change over time
A line graph only works for comparing separate categories

quiz.q: Why is it important to choose the right type of graph for your data?
options: All graphs always show exactly the same information
Choosing a graph type never affects how clear data is
The right graph makes the data easier to understand and compare
This concept has no relevance to math

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Science: Weather: Types of Precipitation
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Science strand: precipitation is water that falls from clouds to the ground in different forms, including rain, snow, sleet, and hail, depending on the temperature of the air it falls through.
subjects.resourceLabel: TVO Learn: Grade 4 Science and Technology

quiz.q: What do we call water that falls from clouds to the ground?
Name one form of precipitation, such as snow.
Is hail a form of precipitation made of small balls of ice?
options: No, hail is never made of ice
Hail only falls as a liquid

quiz.q: Why might rain turn into snow as it falls?
options: The air it falls through can be cold enough to freeze the water
This concept has no relevance to science
Temperature has no connection to the type of precipitation
Rain always stays exactly the same no matter the temperature

quiz.q: Why is sleet sometimes tricky to predict compared to rain or snow?
options: This concept has no relevance to weather
It forms when precipitation partly melts and then refreezes as it falls
Sleet always falls in exactly the same way as snow
Sleet has no connection to temperature changes in the air

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Social Studies: The Group of Seven: Canadian Landscape Painters
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Social Studies strand: the Group of Seven was a collection of Canadian artists in the early twentieth century known for painting Canada’s rugged wilderness landscapes, helping shape a distinct Canadian artistic identity.

quiz.q: What was the Group of Seven known for painting?
options: A concept unrelated to Canadian art

quiz.q: When did the Group of Seven create most of their well-known work?
options: A concept unrelated to Canadian history

quiz.q: Did the Group of Seven help shape a distinct Canadian artistic identity?
options: No, they had no effect on Canadian art
They only painted portraits of world leaders

quiz.q: Why might the Group of Seven have chosen to paint Canada’s wilderness instead of European scenes?
options: European scenes and Canadian wilderness look exactly the same
This concept has no connection to Canadian art
They wanted to capture and celebrate Canada’s own unique landscapes
They had no interest in Canada’s landscapes at all

quiz.q: Why do many Canadians still recognize the Group of Seven’s paintings today?
options: Their paintings have been completely forgotten by everyone
Their artwork remains an important and celebrated part of Canadian culture
This concept has no relevance to social studies
Canadian art has no connection to Canadian identity

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Writing: Writing a Biography
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Language strand: a biography is a true account of a real person’s life written by someone else, often including important events, achievements, and challenges the person experienced.

quiz.q: What do we call a true account of a real person’s life written by someone else?
Should a biography include important events and achievements from the person’s life?
options: No, biographies never include real events
Only fictional events belong in a biography

quiz.q: Is a biography written by the person about their own life, or by someone else?
options: By the person about their own life
Biographies are never written by anyone

quiz.q: Why might a biography include the challenges a person faced, not just their achievements?
options: Challenges have no connection to understanding a person’s life
It gives a fuller, more honest picture of the person’s life
A biography should only ever describe perfect events
This concept has no connection to writing a biography

quiz.q: Which of these would most likely appear in a biography of a famous scientist?
options: A made-up story about a dragon
A description of the discoveries and struggles that shaped their career
A list of unrelated math equations

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Probability: Certain, Impossible, and Equally Likely Events
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Math strand: an event is certain if it will definitely happen, impossible if it can never happen, and equally likely if it has the same chance of happening as another outcome, such as flipping heads or tails on a fair coin.

quiz.q: What do we call an event that will definitely happen?
What do we call an event that can never happen?
Is flipping heads or tails on a fair coin an example of equally likely outcomes?
options: A coin can never land on either side
No, one outcome is always more likely than the other

quiz.q: Why is rolling a 7 on a standard 6-sided die considered impossible?
options: This concept has no connection to probability
Rolling a 7 happens very often on a 6-sided die
Every number is equally likely to appear, including 7
A standard die only has the numbers 1 through 6, so 7 can never appear

quiz.q: Why is it certain that the Sun will rise tomorrow?
options: This event has never actually happened before
This concept has no relevance to probability
The Sun rising is only a possible, not certain, event
It happens every single day without exception, based on the Earth’s rotation

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Science: Rocks and Minerals: Everyday Uses
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Science strand: rocks and minerals are used in many everyday products and structures, such as limestone in buildings, graphite in pencils, and quartz in glass and electronics.
subjects.resourceLabel: TVO Learn: Grade 4 Science and Technology

quiz.q: Name one everyday product made using a mineral, such as pencils made with graphite.
options: A concept unrelated to rocks and minerals

quiz.q: What type of rock is often used in building construction, such as limestone?
options: A concept unrelated to rocks and minerals

quiz.q: Is quartz a mineral used in making glass?
options: Glass is never made using minerals
No, quartz has no connection to glass

quiz.q: Why might it be important to study where useful rocks and minerals come from?
options: This concept has no relevance to science
It helps us understand how to responsibly find and use these natural resources
Rocks and minerals have no connection to everyday products
Useful rocks and minerals never actually run out

quiz.q: Why might electronics companies be interested in minerals like quartz?
options: This concept has no relevance to science
Minerals are never used in making electronics
Certain minerals have properties useful for building electronic components
Electronics are made entirely without any natural materials

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Social Studies: The Role of the Governor General in Canada
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Social Studies strand: the Governor General is the representative of the Crown in Canada, performing ceremonial duties such as opening Parliament and formally approving new laws.

quiz.q: Who does the Governor General represent in Canada?
Name one ceremonial duty of the Governor General, such as opening Parliament.
Does the Governor General formally approve new laws passed by Parliament?
options: No, the Governor General has no role in approving laws
Only mayors approve laws in Canada

quiz.q: Why might the role of the Governor General be considered mostly ceremonial today?
options: The Governor General personally makes every government decision alone
This concept has no connection to Canadian government
Canada has no connection to the Crown at all
Elected leaders, not the Governor General, make most day-to-day political decisions

quiz.q: Why might it be useful for students to learn about the Governor General’s role?
options: Canada’s government has no formal structure at all
The Governor General has no relevance to understanding government
It helps explain how Canada’s system of government is structured
This concept has no relevance to social studies

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Figurative Language: Assonance and Consonance
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Language strand: assonance is the repetition of vowel sounds within nearby words, such as the ee sound in fleet feet, while consonance is the repetition of consonant sounds, such as the t sound in pitter patter.

quiz.q: What do we call the repetition of vowel sounds within nearby words?
options: A concept unrelated to figurative language

quiz.q: What do we call the repetition of consonant sounds within nearby words?
options: A concept unrelated to figurative language

quiz.q: Which phrase is an example of assonance?
options: A concept unrelated to figurative language

quiz.q: Why might a poet use assonance or consonance in their writing?
options: These sound patterns can make a poem more musical and memorable
Sound patterns are never used in poetry
Assonance and consonance never affect how a poem sounds
This concept has no connection to figurative language

quiz.q: Which phrase is an example of consonance, repeating a consonant sound?
options: A concept unrelated to figurative language

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Fractions: Ordering Fractions with Different Denominators
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Math strand: students learn to order fractions with different denominators from least to greatest by finding a common denominator or comparing them to a benchmark like one half.

quiz.q: To order fractions with different denominators, what can you find to help compare them?
Which fraction is greater, 1 half or 1 third?
Which list correctly orders these fractions from least to greatest: 1 fourth, 1 half, 3 fourths?
Why is one half often used as a helpful benchmark when comparing fractions?
options: Benchmarks never help with comparing numbers
It gives an easy reference point to decide if a fraction is bigger or smaller
This concept has no connection to ordering fractions
One half can never be used to compare fractions

quiz.q: Which fraction is closest to one whole: 1 fourth, 1 half, or 3 fourths?
options: They are all exactly equal to one whole

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Science: Simple Machines: The Wedge
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Science strand: a wedge is a simple machine with a thick end that tapers to a thin edge, used to push things apart or cut through materials, such as an axe blade or a doorstop.
subjects.resourceLabel: TVO Learn: Grade 4 Science and Technology

quiz.q: What shape does a wedge have, with a thick end tapering to what?
options: A concept unrelated to simple machines

quiz.q: Name one example of a wedge, such as an axe blade.
options: A concept unrelated to simple machines

quiz.q: Can a wedge be used to push things apart or cut through materials?
options: No, a wedge is never used for cutting or splitting
A wedge can only be used to hold a door open forever

quiz.q: Why might a doorstop shaped like a wedge work well to hold a door in place?
options: This concept has no relevance to science
Its tapered shape can wedge tightly against the floor and door
A doorstop shaped like a wedge can never actually work
A wedge shape has no connection to holding objects in place

quiz.q: Why is a wedge considered a simple machine, similar to a lever or pulley?
options: This concept has no connection to simple machines
It changes the amount or direction of force needed to do work
Simple machines never make any task easier
A wedge never changes how much force is needed

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Social Studies: Public Services and Community Institutions
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Social Studies strand: public services such as libraries, hospitals, and fire departments are funded by government and community resources to meet the needs of everyone in a community.

quiz.q: Name one public service that meets a community’s needs, such as a library.
options: A concept unrelated to community services

quiz.q: Are public services like hospitals typically funded by government and community resources?
options: A concept unrelated to public services
Public services are always fully paid for by one single family
No, public services are never funded by the community

quiz.q: What public service responds quickly to fires and emergencies?
options: A concept unrelated to public services

quiz.q: Why might a community choose to fund a public library instead of leaving it to private businesses?
options: This concept has no connection to public services
It ensures that books and resources are accessible to everyone, regardless of income
Only wealthy people are ever allowed to use a library
Public libraries have no benefit to a community

quiz.q: Why are public services like hospitals and fire departments important to a community?
options: Communities never actually need this kind of support
This concept has no relevance to social studies
They provide essential care and safety that benefit all community members
Public services have no real impact on a community

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Grammar: Modal Verbs
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Language strand: modal verbs, such as can, could, should, and must, are helping verbs that express ability, possibility, permission, or necessity.

quiz.q: What do we call helping verbs such as can, could, should, and must?
Which modal verb expresses ability, as in I can swim?
Which modal verb expresses necessity, as in You must wear a helmet?
Why might a writer use should instead of must in a sentence giving advice?
options: Should and must always mean exactly the same thing
Should suggests a recommendation, while must suggests a requirement
Modal verbs never change the meaning of a sentence
This concept has no connection to grammar

quiz.q: Which sentence correctly uses a modal verb to ask for permission?

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Financial Literacy: Understanding Sales Tax (GST/HST)
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Math strand: sales tax is an extra percentage added to the price of many goods and services, such as the HST in Ontario, which increases the total amount a customer pays at checkout.

quiz.q: What do we call the extra percentage added to the price of many goods and services?
What is the name of the sales tax charged in Ontario?
Does sales tax increase or decrease the total amount a customer pays?
options: A concept unrelated to sales tax
Sales tax has no effect on the total

quiz.q: Why might the price shown on a store shelf be different from the amount you actually pay at checkout?
options: Store prices never change at checkout
This concept has no connection to sales tax
Sales tax always lowers the amount you pay
Sales tax is often added to the shelf price at checkout

quiz.q: If an item costs 10 dollars and 1 dollar 30 cents in sales tax is added, what is the total cost?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Science: Space: The Moon’s Effect on Earth — Tides
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Science strand: the Moon’s gravity pulls on Earth’s oceans, causing tides, the regular rising and falling of sea levels along coastlines.
subjects.resourceLabel: TVO Learn: Grade 4 Science and Technology

quiz.q: What causes tides, the regular rising and falling of sea levels?
options: The Moon’s gravity pulling on Earth’s oceans
The Sun’s heat warming the oceans
Wind blowing across the ocean surface only

quiz.q: Do tides cause sea levels along coastlines to rise and fall?
options: Tides only affect rivers, never oceans

quiz.q: What force from the Moon is mainly responsible for tides?
Why might coastal communities need to know the tide schedule?
options: This concept has no relevance to science
Tide schedules never change and do not need tracking
Tides can affect boating, fishing, and beach safety
Tides have no connection to coastal communities

quiz.q: Why does the Moon, despite being far away, have a noticeable effect on Earth’s oceans?
options: The Moon has no gravitational pull at all
Distance always makes gravity completely disappear
Its gravitational pull is strong enough to move huge amounts of ocean water
This concept has no relevance to science

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Social Studies: The Role of Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs)
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Social Studies strand: a non-governmental organization, or NGO, is a group that is not run by the government and works to help people or protect the environment, such as providing disaster relief or supporting education.

quiz.q: What does NGO stand for?
Is an NGO run by the government?
options: Yes, NGOs are always run by the government
NGOs are the same thing as a national government

quiz.q: Name one type of work an NGO might do, such as disaster relief.
Why might people choose to support an NGO that helps with education in other countries?
options: It can help provide resources and opportunities where they are needed most
This concept has no connection to global issues
Education has no connection to community wellbeing

quiz.q: Why might NGOs work alongside governments instead of replacing them?
options: NGOs can offer additional support and expertise that complements government efforts
NGOs and governments never work together
NGOs always try to replace a country’s government
This concept has no relevance to social studies

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Reading: Understanding Flashback in Narratives
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Language strand: a flashback is when a story pauses its current events to show something that happened earlier, helping readers understand a character’s past or the reasons behind their actions.

quiz.q: What do we call it when a story pauses to show something that happened earlier?
Can a flashback help readers understand a character’s past?
options: No, flashbacks never explain anything about a character

quiz.q: Does a flashback show events from before or after the current point in the story?
options: Flashbacks show no events at all

quiz.q: Why might an author use a flashback to explain why a character is afraid of dogs?
options: A character’s fear can never be explained by past events
This concept has no connection to storytelling
Flashbacks never explain a character’s feelings
It can reveal a past experience that caused the character’s fear

quiz.q: Which of these is most likely a signal that a flashback is beginning in a story?
options: The story ends with no further explanation
A concept unrelated to the story
A character orders food at a restaurant
The narrator suddenly describes an event from years earlier

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Geometry: Perimeter of Regular Polygons Using Formulas
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Math strand: students learn to find the perimeter of a regular polygon, where all sides are equal, by multiplying the length of one side by the number of sides.

quiz.q: How do you find the perimeter of a regular polygon?
options: Add the length of only two sides
Multiply the length of one side by the number of sides
Divide the length of one side by the number of sides

quiz.q: What is the perimeter of a regular pentagon with sides of 6 cm?
What is the perimeter of a square with sides of 8 cm?
Why does multiplying work as a shortcut for finding the perimeter of a regular polygon?
options: Multiplication never works for finding perimeter
This concept has no connection to geometry
All the sides are equal, so adding them repeatedly is the same as multiplying
Regular polygons never have equal sides

quiz.q: What is the perimeter of a regular hexagon with sides of 5 cm?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Science: Forces: Streamlining and Reducing Drag in Water
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Science strand: streamlining is shaping an object to move smoothly through water with less resistance, called drag, which is why boats and fish often have narrow, smooth, pointed shapes.
subjects.resourceLabel: TVO Learn: Grade 4 Science and Technology

quiz.q: What do we call the force that resists an object’s motion through water?
What do we call shaping an object to move smoothly through water with less resistance?
Do fish often have narrow, smooth, pointed shapes to help them move through water?
options: Fish are always shaped like perfect cubes
No, fish shapes have no connection to moving through water

quiz.q: Why might boat designers shape a hull to be narrow and smooth?
options: A wide, blocky shape always moves fastest through water
A streamlined shape reduces drag, helping the boat move faster and more efficiently
This concept has no relevance to science
Boat shape has no connection to how fast it can travel

quiz.q: Why might a wide, flat object experience more drag moving through water than a narrow, pointed one?
options: It pushes against more water at once, increasing resistance
This concept has no relevance to forces
Flat objects always move through water with no resistance at all
Shape never affects how much resistance an object experiences

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Social Studies: Canada’s Agricultural Regions and Food Production
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Social Studies strand: Canada has distinct agricultural regions, such as the Prairies for grain farming and the Niagara region for fruit growing, which produce much of the country’s food.

quiz.q: Which Canadian region is known for grain farming, such as wheat?
Which Ontario region is known for growing fruit, such as grapes and peaches?
Do different regions of Canada grow different types of food based on their climate and land?
options: Climate never affects what can be grown
No, every region of Canada grows exactly the same food

quiz.q: Why might farmers in the Prairies focus on growing grain crops like wheat?
options: This concept has no relevance to social studies
The flat land and climate of the Prairies are well suited to grain farming
The Prairies have no connection to farming
Grain crops can only be grown in mountains

quiz.q: Why is it important for Canada to have multiple agricultural regions producing different foods?
options: This concept has no relevance to social studies
Having multiple agricultural regions has no benefit to Canada
Canada does not need to grow its own food
It helps provide a variety of food and supports the country’s food supply

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Writing: Writing an Interview
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Language strand: writing an interview involves preparing clear questions in advance and recording a person’s answers accurately, often used in non-fiction writing like newspaper articles.

quiz.q: What should a writer prepare in advance before conducting an interview?
options: A drawing with no connection to the topic

quiz.q: What is the goal of recording a person’s answers during an interview?
options: To ignore what the person actually says
To make up new answers instead

quiz.q: Is an interview often used in non-fiction writing, like newspaper articles?
options: No, interviews are never used in non-fiction writing
Interviews are only used in fictional stories

quiz.q: Why might a reporter ask open-ended questions during an interview instead of only yes-or-no questions?
options: This concept has no connection to interviewing
Yes-or-no questions always provide the most detail
Open-ended questions can lead to more detailed and useful answers
Open-ended questions never provide any useful information

quiz.q: Which of these would most likely be a good interview question for a local firefighter?
options: What made you want to become a firefighter?
What is the chemical symbol for water?

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Number Sense: Divisibility Rules (2, 5, and 10)
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Math strand: divisibility rules help determine if a number can be divided evenly by another number, such as a number being divisible by 2 if it ends in an even digit, by 5 if it ends in 0 or 5, and by 10 if it ends in 0.

quiz.q: A number is divisible by 2 if it ends in what kind of digit?
A number is divisible by 5 if it ends in which digits?
A number is divisible by 10 if it ends in which digit?
Why are divisibility rules useful when working with larger numbers?
options: Divisibility rules never help with checking numbers
This concept has no connection to number sense
Divisibility rules only work for very small numbers
They let you quickly check if a number divides evenly without doing full division

quiz.q: Is the number 340 divisible by both 2 and 5?
options: No, 340 is divisible by neither 2 nor 5
340 is divisible by 5 only, never by 2

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Science: Ecosystems: Keystone Species
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Science strand: a keystone species is a species that has a very large effect on its ecosystem compared to its numbers, such as a sea otter controlling sea urchin populations to protect kelp forests.
subjects.resourceLabel: TVO Learn: Grade 4 Science and Technology

quiz.q: What do we call a species that has a very large effect on its ecosystem compared to its numbers?
Name one example of a keystone species, such as the sea otter.
options: A concept unrelated to keystone species

quiz.q: Can removing a keystone species cause major changes throughout an ecosystem?
options: Keystone species have no real impact on their ecosystem
No, removing any species never affects an ecosystem

quiz.q: Why might sea otters be important for protecting kelp forests?
options: Kelp forests grow better without any otters present
Sea otters have no connection to kelp forests
This concept has no relevance to science
They control sea urchin populations that would otherwise overeat the kelp

quiz.q: Why do scientists pay special attention to keystone species when protecting an ecosystem?
options: Losing a keystone species can cause much bigger changes than losing other species
Keystone species are never actually important to an ecosystem
This concept has no relevance to science
All species have exactly the same impact on an ecosystem

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Social Studies: The St. Lawrence Seaway and Its Importance to Trade
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Social Studies strand: the St. Lawrence Seaway is a system of canals and locks that allows large ships to travel between the Atlantic Ocean and the Great Lakes, supporting trade across central Canada.

quiz.q: What is the St. Lawrence Seaway a system of?
What does the St. Lawrence Seaway allow large ships to travel between?
options: Two cities within the same province
The Pacific Ocean and the Arctic Ocean
The Atlantic Ocean and the Great Lakes

quiz.q: Does the St. Lawrence Seaway support trade across central Canada?
options: The Seaway only supports trade in one small town
A concept unrelated to the Seaway
No, the Seaway has no connection to trade

quiz.q: Why are locks an important part of the St. Lawrence Seaway?
options: They raise and lower ships to different water levels along the route
This concept has no relevance to social studies
Locks are only used to stop ships from moving at all
Locks have no function within the Seaway

quiz.q: Why might the St. Lawrence Seaway be considered important to Canada’s economy?
options: This concept has no relevance to social studies
Goods can never be transported using the Seaway
The Seaway has no connection to Canada’s economy
It allows goods to be shipped efficiently between inland regions and international markets

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Review: Vocabulary, Grammar, and Reading Skills (Days 91-99)
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Language strand review: students revisit verb tenses, foreshadowing, shades of meaning, sentence fragments and run-ons, biography writing, assonance and consonance, modal verbs, flashback, and interview writing.

quiz.q: Which verb tense describes an action that already happened?
What do we call words with similar meanings but different levels of intensity, like happy, glad, and thrilled?
What do we call a true account of a real person’s life written by someone else?
What do we call helping verbs such as can, could, should, and must?
What should a writer prepare in advance before conducting an interview?
options: A drawing with no connection to the topic

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Review: Fractions, Number Sense, and Data (Days 91-99)
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Math strand review: students revisit subtracting mixed numbers, expanded form, adding and subtracting money, choosing appropriate graphs, probability terms, ordering fractions, sales tax, perimeter of regular polygons, and divisibility rules.

quiz.q: What is 3 and 3 fourths minus 1 and 1 fourth?
What is 4 dollars 25 cents plus 2 dollars 50 cents?
What do we call an event that will definitely happen?
What do we call the extra percentage added to the price of many goods and services?
A number is divisible by 2 if it ends in what kind of digit?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Review: Structures, Matter, Energy, and Earth and Space Systems (Days 91-99)
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Science strand review: students revisit shell/frame/solid structures, separating mixtures, potential and kinetic energy, precipitation, rocks and minerals, the wedge, tides, drag in water, and keystone species.
subjects.resourceLabel: TVO Learn: Grade 4 Science and Technology

quiz.q: What do we call a structure with a strong outer covering and a hollow inside?
What do we call stored energy an object has because of its position?
Name one everyday product made using a mineral, such as pencils made with graphite.
options: A concept unrelated to rocks and minerals

quiz.q: What causes tides, the regular rising and falling of sea levels?
options: Wind blowing across the ocean surface only
The Sun’s heat warming the oceans
The Moon’s gravity pulling on Earth’s oceans

quiz.q: What do we call a species that has a very large effect on its ecosystem compared to its numbers?

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Review: Ancient Societies, Government, and Canadian Geography (Days 91-99)
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Social Studies strand review: students revisit the Inca civilization, the Constitution and Charter, federal elections, the Group of Seven, the Governor General, public services, NGOs, agricultural regions, and the St. Lawrence Seaway.

quiz.q: Where did the Inca civilization build its empire?
options: A concept unrelated to ancient civilizations
Along the Andes Mountains in South America

quiz.q: How old must a Canadian citizen typically be to vote in a federal election?
Who does the Governor General represent in Canada?
What does NGO stand for?
What is the St. Lawrence Seaway a system of?

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Grammar: Types of Nouns — Common, Proper, Collective, and Abstract
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Language strand: nouns can be common (a general name like city), proper (a specific name like Toronto, always capitalized), collective (a name for a group like team or flock), or abstract (a name for an idea or feeling like happiness).

quiz.q: What do we call a noun that names a specific person, place, or thing and starts with a capital letter?
What do we call a noun that names a group of people or things, such as team or flock?
What do we call a noun that names an idea or feeling, such as happiness or courage?
Why is dog considered a common noun while Rex is a proper noun?
options: Rex names one specific dog, while dog names any dog in general
Common nouns and proper nouns always mean exactly the same thing
This concept has no connection to grammar

quiz.q: Which sentence correctly uses a collective noun?
options: The herds of cow walked across field cows.
The herd of cows walked across the field.
Herd cow the field walked across.

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Number Sense: Even and Odd Numbers and Their Properties
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Math strand: even numbers can be split into two equal groups and end in 0, 2, 4, 6, or 8, while odd numbers cannot be split evenly and end in 1, 3, 5, 7, or 9.

quiz.q: Which digit endings always make a number even?
options: A concept unrelated to number sense

quiz.q: Is the number 47 even or odd?
options: A concept unrelated to number sense

quiz.q: When you add two even numbers, such as 4 plus 6, is the sum always even?
options: The sum is never a whole number
A concept unrelated to number sense
No, the sum is always odd

quiz.q: Why is 8 considered an even number?
options: 8 has no relationship to grouping at all
It can be split into two equal groups with none left over
It can never be divided evenly
This concept has no connection to number sense

quiz.q: Which of these numbers is odd?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Science: Structures: Forces Acting on Structures — Tension and Compression
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Science strand: structures experience tension, a pulling or stretching force, and compression, a pushing or squeezing force, and engineers design structures to withstand both.
subjects.resourceLabel: TVO Learn: Grade 4 Science and Technology

quiz.q: What do we call a pulling or stretching force on a structure?
What do we call a pushing or squeezing force on a structure?
Is a rope holding up a swing under tension as it stretches?
options: No, ropes are never under tension

quiz.q: Why must engineers design bridges to withstand both tension and compression?
options: Different parts of a bridge experience different forces as weight moves across it
This concept has no connection to structures
Bridges never experience any forces at all
Tension and compression never occur in real structures

quiz.q: Which example shows compression?
options: A kite string blowing loosely in the wind
A rope being stretched by a swing
A stack of books pressing down on a table

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Social Studies: The Indus Valley Civilization
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Social Studies strand: the Indus Valley civilization developed along the Indus River in present-day Pakistan and India, known for well-planned cities such as Mohenjo-daro that had organized streets and drainage systems.

quiz.q: Along which river did the Indus Valley civilization develop?
options: A concept unrelated to ancient civilizations

quiz.q: Name a well-planned city built by the Indus Valley civilization.
options: A concept unrelated to the Indus Valley

quiz.q: Was the Indus Valley civilization known for organized city planning, including drainage systems?
options: No, its cities had no planning at all
A concept unrelated to the Indus Valley
Its cities had no streets or buildings

quiz.q: Why do historians consider the Indus Valley’s drainage systems impressive?
options: This concept has no relevance to social studies
They show advanced engineering skill for such an early civilization
The Indus Valley civilization never built anything at all
Drainage systems have no connection to ancient engineering

quiz.q: In which present-day countries was the Indus Valley civilization located?
options: A concept unrelated to the Indus Valley

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Reading: Drawing Conclusions
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Language strand: drawing conclusions means using clues from the text along with what you already know to figure out something the author does not state directly.

quiz.q: What do we call using text clues and prior knowledge to figure out something an author does not state directly?
If a character grabs an umbrella and puts on rain boots before leaving, what conclusion can you draw?
options: It is a sunny, dry day
A concept unrelated to the story
Nothing at all can be concluded
It is probably raining or about to rain

quiz.q: Do readers usually need clues from the text to draw an accurate conclusion?
options: No, conclusions never need any evidence
Conclusions are always stated directly by the author

quiz.q: Why might drawing conclusions make reading more enjoyable?
options: Drawing conclusions never adds anything to reading
Conclusions always ruin the ending of a story
It lets readers think more deeply and make connections beyond the words on the page
This concept has no connection to reading

quiz.q: If a character’s hands are shaking and their face is pale before a test, what conclusion can you draw?
options: They are probably nervous or scared
A concept unrelated to the story

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Number Sense: Negative Numbers in Everyday Contexts
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Math strand: negative numbers are numbers less than zero, often used to describe situations such as temperatures below freezing or an elevation below sea level.

quiz.q: What do we call a number less than zero?
options: A concept unrelated to number sense

quiz.q: Which temperature is colder: -5 degrees or 2 degrees?
On a number line, negative numbers are found in which direction from zero?
options: A concept unrelated to number sense

quiz.q: Why is it useful to represent temperatures below freezing with negative numbers?
options: Negative numbers never represent real values
Temperatures can never actually go below zero
This concept has no connection to number sense
It gives an accurate way to show values that are less than zero

quiz.q: Which of these numbers is the coldest?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Science: Earth and Space: Layers of the Earth
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Science strand: Earth is made of layers, including the crust we live on, the mantle beneath it, and the core at the very centre, which has an outer and an inner part.
subjects.resourceLabel: TVO Learn: Grade 4 Science and Technology

quiz.q: What is the name of Earth’s outermost layer, where we live?
options: A concept unrelated to Earth’s layers

quiz.q: Which layer lies directly beneath the crust?
options: A concept unrelated to Earth’s layers

quiz.q: What is the name of the layer at the very centre of the Earth?
options: A concept unrelated to Earth’s layers

quiz.q: Why is the crust often compared to the thin skin of an apple?
options: This concept has no connection to Earth’s layers
The crust and the core are exactly the same thickness
It is a very thin layer compared to the much thicker layers beneath it
The crust is actually the thickest layer of the Earth

quiz.q: Does Earth’s core have both an outer and an inner part?
options: No, the core has only one part
A concept unrelated to Earth’s layers
The core has no connection to Earth’s structure

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Social Studies: Ancient Kingdoms of West Africa — Ghana, Mali, and Songhai
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Social Studies strand: the West African kingdoms of Ghana, Mali, and Songhai grew wealthy and powerful through trade in gold and salt across the Sahara Desert.

quiz.q: Name one ancient kingdom of West Africa.
options: A concept unrelated to West Africa

quiz.q: What resources helped these West African kingdoms grow wealthy?
Across which desert did much of this trade take place?
options: A concept unrelated to West Africa

quiz.q: Why might control of trade routes have helped these kingdoms grow powerful?
options: Trade routes never affected how powerful a kingdom became
It let them profit from and manage the flow of valuable goods like gold and salt
These kingdoms had no connection to trade at all
This concept has no connection to West African history

quiz.q: Were Ghana, Mali, and Songhai all located in the same general region of Africa?
options: They were located only in modern-day Canada
No, they were located on different continents
A concept unrelated to West Africa

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Grammar: Relative Pronouns — Who, Which, and That
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Language strand: relative pronouns such as who, which, and that connect a describing clause to a noun, with who used for people and which or that often used for things.

quiz.q: Which relative pronoun is typically used to describe people?
Which sentence correctly uses a relative pronoun?
options: Borrowed I book that excellent was.
The book that I borrowed was excellent.
The book who I borrowed was excellent.

quiz.q: Which relative pronoun would best complete: The scientist ___ discovered the cure was famous.
Why do writers use relative pronouns like who and which?
options: This concept has no connection to grammar
Relative pronouns never add any information to a sentence
Relative pronouns always begin a brand new sentence
They connect extra descriptive information to a noun in the same sentence

quiz.q: Which relative pronoun usually describes a thing rather than a person?
options: Whom is only used for animals

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Algebra: Solving Equations with an Unknown Value
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Math strand: students learn to find the value of an unknown, often shown as a letter or symbol, in a simple equation, such as solving n plus 5 equals 12 for n.

quiz.q: What is the value of n in n plus 5 equals 12?
What is the value of x in x minus 3 equals 9?
What is the value of y in y times 4 equals 20?
Why is it helpful to check your answer by substituting it back into the equation?
options: It confirms whether the value makes the equation true
Substituting a value always makes an equation false
This concept has no connection to algebra
Checking an answer never actually proves anything

quiz.q: What is the value of a in 6 plus a equals 14?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Science: Light: Opaque, Translucent, and Transparent Materials
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Science strand: materials can be transparent, letting light pass through clearly like glass; translucent, letting some light through like wax paper; or opaque, blocking light completely like wood.
subjects.resourceLabel: TVO Learn: Grade 4 Science and Technology

quiz.q: What do we call a material that lets light pass through clearly, like glass?
What do we call a material that blocks light completely?
What do we call a material that lets some light through but blurs what is on the other side, like wax paper?
Why are windows usually made from a transparent material?
options: This concept has no connection to light
Transparent materials always block all light
Windows are never made from transparent materials
It allows light and a clear view to pass through easily

quiz.q: Which of these is a good example of an opaque material?

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Social Studies: United Empire Loyalists
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Social Studies strand: United Empire Loyalists were colonists who remained loyal to Britain during the American Revolution and moved north to settle in what is now Canada.

quiz.q: Who were the United Empire Loyalists?
options: Colonists who remained loyal to Britain and moved to Canada
A concept unrelated to Canadian history

quiz.q: During which conflict did many Loyalists move north to Canada?
Did the arrival of Loyalists help shape early settlement in what is now Canada?
options: No, Loyalists had no effect on early Canada

quiz.q: Why is the resettlement of Loyalists in areas like present-day Ontario significant?
options: This concept has no connection to Canadian history
Population growth has no connection to settlement
Loyalists never settled in what is now Ontario
It contributed to population growth and the development of new communities

quiz.q: Where did most Loyalists live before moving north?

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Reading: Understanding Irony
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Language strand: irony occurs when what actually happens is different from what a reader or character expects, often creating humour or surprise.

quiz.q: What do we call it when what happens is different from what is expected?
If a fire station burns down, what makes this situation ironic?
options: A place meant to fight fires was destroyed by one
The fire station is very old
Fire stations always catch fire eventually
A concept unrelated to the story

quiz.q: Can irony create humour or surprise for a reader?
options: No, irony never creates any reaction
Irony always makes a story boring

quiz.q: Why might an author use irony in a story?
options: It can add surprise or humour by showing an unexpected outcome
Irony never affects how a reader feels
Authors should never include unexpected outcomes
This concept has no connection to storytelling

quiz.q: Which situation is an example of irony?
options: A concept unrelated to the story
A baker bakes bread every day.
A traffic officer gets a parking ticket.
A student studies hard and passes a test.

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Measurement: Area of Rectangles Using a Formula
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Math strand: students learn to find the area of a rectangle by multiplying its length by its width, with the result measured in square units.

quiz.q: What formula finds the area of a rectangle?
What is the area of a rectangle with a length of 6 cm and a width of 4 cm?
What is the area of a rectangle with a length of 9 cm and a width of 3 cm?
Why is area measured in square units, such as square centimetres?
options: Area is always measured using only whole numbers with no units
This concept has no connection to measurement
It describes how much flat, two-dimensional surface a shape covers
Square units never actually describe any surface

quiz.q: What is the area of a square with sides of 5 cm?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Science: Simple Machines: Fixed and Movable Pulleys
subjects.summary: Grade 4 Science strand: a fixed pulley is attached in one place and changes the direction of a force, while a movable pulley moves with the load and helps reduce the force needed to lift it.
subjects.resourceLabel: TVO Learn: Grade 4 Science and Technology

quiz.q: What type of pulley stays attached in one place and changes the direction of a force?
options: A concept unrelated to simple machines

quiz.q: What type of pulley moves with the load and helps reduce the force needed to lift it?
options: A concept unrelated to simple machines

quiz.q: Does a flag pole typically use a fixed pulley to raise the flag?
options: No, flag poles never use pulleys
Flag poles only use movable pulleys

quiz.q: Why might a movable pulley make it easier to lift a heavy load?
options: Movable pulleys never actually help lift anything
This concept has no connection to simple machines
A movable pulley always makes an object heavier
It can reduce the amount of force needed compared to lifting the load directly

quiz.q: Which of these is an example of a fixed pulley in use?
